finding: The filesystem peer-terminal journal could leave an unrecoverable lock directory when its process died after acquiring the lock. A successor now reads a PID-bound owner record, confirms the owner process is dead, atomically quarantines the stale lock directory, removes it, and retries acquisition.
design_implication: Durable terminal recording requires recovery semantics for process death, not only exception handling inside one process. The stored journal remains authoritative after rename; a successor must preserve an already-renamed terminal and only replace a terminal when the crash occurred before rename.

verification.method: source inspection plus committed child-process SIGKILL tests

uncertainty: No clean-runner result was available for the new commits.
PID reuse between owner death and recovery is theoretically possible; a future lease should include a process-start identity where the target platform exposes one portably.
Filesystem and directory-sync guarantees vary by operating system and filesystem.
falsification_route: Run the committed Node test on Linux and macOS; any lock timeout after the injected SIGKILL falsifies recovery.
Verify that before-rename crashes permit the successor terminal and post-rename crashes preserve the first terminal.
Stress repeated crash/restart cycles and assert no .lock or .stale directories remain.
privacy: No private chat, health material, credentials, or user-identifying data included.
